result:
:::writing
At some point, every city learns how to hum.

Not the loud kind—the obvious noise of traffic, construction, people shouting into their phones—but a quieter, steadier sound that lives underneath everything else. It’s in the way streetlights flicker on just before dusk, each one a tiny agreement to keep going. It’s in the rhythm of footsteps crossing the same intersection day after day, strangers unknowingly syncing their routines.

If you stand still long enough, you can feel it. The barista who already knows someone’s order before they speak. The bus driver who waits an extra second because he recognizes a familiar face running down the block. Even the stray cat that appears at the same corner every evening, as if clocking in for a shift no one assigned.

Cities pretend to be chaotic, but they’re full of quiet patterns. Invisible threads. People brushing past each other, carrying entire worlds that briefly overlap and then drift apart again. A thousand almost-conversations. A million unnoticed kindnesses.

And then, every so often, something breaks the rhythm. A sudden downpour. A power outage. A shared moment where everyone looks up at the same time. For a second, the hum wavers—and you realize it was there all along, holding everything together.

Then it resumes, softer than before, but steadier.

Like a promise no one remembers making, but everyone keeps.
:::

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
